_meta.purpose: Concept-based semantic index for deterministic retrieval. Every unique area of the 2026 AHA/ASA AIS Guidelines is hand-labeled with a concept + meaning. Step 1 (LLM) maps question -> concepts. Step 3 (Python) does dict lookup concept -> units. Step 4 (LLM) reads meaning to compose the answer.
_meta.guideline: 2026 AHA/ASA Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke

schema.concept: snake_case machine handle. Matches a specific clinical decision point, not a topic.
schema.meaning: Prose the LLM reads to understand what this unit answers.
schema.id: Stable identifier: rec.<section>.<n>, rss.<section>.<n>, kg.<section>[.<n>], syn.<section>, table.<n>, table.<n>.row.<k>, figure.<n>
schema.section: Section number the unit lives in
schema.supports_rec: For RSS units, the rec.<id> the RSS supports (evidence back-pointer)
schema.concepts: For multi-decision units, multiple concept handles in a list

labeling_rules: One concept answers ONE distinct clinical question.
RSS entries inherit the paired rec's concept and carry supports_rec.
Concept names reflect the clinical decision point, not the topic (e.g. post_ivt_bp_target_180_105_first_24h, not bp_management).
COR=3:Harm and COR=3:No Benefit recs get -harmful/-no_benefit suffixes in their concept to make negative recommendations retrievable.
Concept values are stable across versions - if the guideline changes wording, the concept stays, the meaning updates.
A unit may carry multiple concepts (concepts[] list) when it covers >1 decision point.

section_2.title: Stroke Systems of Care and Prehospital Management

2.1.meaning: Whether/how public and professional education programs should be implemented to speed stroke recognition and 911 activation.

units.meaning: Public stroke-recognition education programs are recommended for the general public to improve 911 activation and reduce knowledge gaps.
Public stroke-education programs should be designed to reach diverse communities to close demographic knowledge gaps.
Stroke-recognition education should be sustained over time because knowledge decays after single interventions.
EMS professionals, physicians, and other health-care personnel should receive targeted stroke-education programs to reduce prehospital delays and maximize thrombolysis eligibility.

Evidence: RCT and nonrandomized studies show short-term gains in stroke knowledge and preparedness after educational interventions in children and adults, including school-based programs; some show behavior change like increased EMS use.

Evidence: persistent inequities in hospital arrival, EMS use, and treatment rates by race/age/ethnicity; culturally tailored interventions (e.g. RAPIDO) improve knowledge and preparedness among at-risk groups.
Evidence: studies tracking retention show stroke knowledge is not maintained long-term; optimal booster timing unknown.
Evidence: interventions targeting prehospital and outpatient clinicians increase EMS use, stroke recognition, and prenotification.
Public stroke awareness (knowledge of warning signs) and preparedness (knowing to call 911) are critical to reducing delays in acute stroke care.

2.2.meaning: How regional EMS and stroke-system agencies should be organized, triaged, and audited to deliver time-sensitive stroke therapies.

units.meaning: Health-care policy makers should establish regional systems of stroke care (ASRH/PSC/TSC/CSC) with defined transport pathways.
EMS leaders should develop prehospital triage protocols with validated screening tools to route suspected stroke to the most appropriate center.
Monitoring and feedback on prehospital quality metrics can be useful to reduce onset-to-reperfusion times and improve discharge home rates.
Evidence: coordinated SSOC with tiered hospital designations and transport agreements accelerate AIS care delivery.
Evidence: prehospital triage and screening tool choice should be set at the regional level as part of a comprehensive approach.

Evidence: prehospital quality metrics (dispatch <=90s, stroke screen, glucose, LKW, scene <=15 min, prenotification, IV line) correlate with faster in-hospital reperfusion and higher discharge home rates. With the exception of scene time <=15 min, metrics also apply to MSUs.
More evidence needed for coordinated regionalized stroke care and for prehospital protocolization with checklists and linked prehospital/hospital records.
Prehospital coordination is crucial for timely AIS treatment; regional stroke bodies must organize routing protocols equitably across the region.

2.3.meaning: How 911 dispatch and EMS should identify and manage suspected stroke in the field, including which treatments to start or avoid before hospital arrival.

units.meaning: EMS dispatchers can use a telephone stroke-assessment tool to speed identification and prioritize transport.
Prehospital personnel should use a brief validated stroke-assessment tool (including LVO screening) for ambulance-transported patients.
Prehospital personnel should prenotify the receiving hospital of an inbound suspected stroke to reduce DTI, increase thrombolytic use, and lower mortality.
Ambulance-initiated remote ischemic conditioning with a BP cuff does NOT improve functional outcome and is not recommended.
Prehospital transdermal glyceryl trinitrate (nitroglycerin) does NOT improve functional outcome and is potentially harmful.
Prehospital intensive BP control to 130-140 systolic does NOT improve functional outcome.
In pediatric patients, adult prehospital stroke screening tools perform poorly and newer pediatric tools are of uncertain usefulness in the field.
Evidence: 2024 systematic review of 24 studies - dispatcher stroke sensitivity 41-83% with overall systems; 48-80% with stroke-specific scales (CPSS/FAST/MPDS).
Evidence: 2019 Cochrane review - LAPSS sensitivity 0.83 specificity 0.93; CPSS sensitivity 0.89 specificity 0.69. Newer LVO tools (PRESTO 2021) identify anterior LVO.
Evidence: GWTG-Stroke data on 371,988 patients - prenotification improved door-to-imaging (26 vs 31 min), DTN (78 vs 80 min), and tPA rate (82.8% vs 79.2%).
Evidence: RESIST trial (Denmark, 1500 patients) - prehospital RIC via upper-extremity cuff did not improve 90-day mRS vs sham.
Evidence: MR ASAP and RIGHT-2 trials - prehospital transdermal GTN did not improve 90-day mRS; safety signals warrant the harm classification.
Evidence: prehospital intensive SBP lowering (target 130-140) did not improve functional outcome.
Evidence: adult screening tools perform poorly in children; pediatric tools show interrater reliability but lack prehospital validation.
EMS professionals play a crucial role in early identification and field management of suspected stroke.

2.4.meaning: Which hospital suspected-stroke patients should be routed to, including LVO bypass decisions and interhospital transfer protocols.

units.meaning: EMS should transport suspected AIS to the closest appropriate stroke-capable facility (ASRH/PSC/TSC/CSC) vs a non-stroke hospital.
For suspected LVO, direct transport to a TSC can be beneficial to increase EVT rates and reduce time to treatment vs non-TSC with secondary transfer.
In areas WITHOUT well-coordinated SSOC, direct transport of suspected LVO to the closest TSC may be reasonable if it does not disqualify from IVT.
In areas WITH well-coordinated SSOC and efficient thrombolysis/transfer, direct transport to a distant TSC does NOT improve 3-month outcomes vs local stroke center.
Hospitals and EMS should establish agreements to prioritize interhospital transfer of acute stroke patients needing higher-level care, to reduce door-in-door-out times.
Evidence: Chicago pre-EVT triage policies directing patients with suspected stroke to stroke-capable centers improved outcomes.
Evidence: Chicago pre/post implementation - EVT rate rose after rerouting suspected LVO directly to CSC.
Evidence: meta-analysis of 18 studies (n=7017) in settings without local TSC access shows uncertain benefit; RACECAT and TRIAGE-STROKE did not cover this.
Evidence: RACECAT (Catalonia, 1401 LVO patients) - direct distant-TSC transport did not improve 90-day mRS; lower IVT rates, higher EVT rates, same mortality.
Evidence: US registry of 108,913 interhospital transfers - median DIDO 174 min; only 27.3% met the 120-min target; shorter DIDO with prenotification and higher NIHSS.
EMS destination planning incorporates transport time, geography, patient/stroke characteristics, and hospital stroke capabilities.

2.5.meaning: When and how to deploy mobile stroke units (specialized ambulances that diagnose and treat AIS including IVT before hospital arrival).

units.meaning: MSUs are recommended over conventional EMS when available for thrombolytic-eligible patients to ensure fastest onset-to-treatment and improve functional outcomes.
MSUs must be equipped to diagnose and treat patients with IVT.
MSU care with streamlined protocols and in-person or remote neurological expertise is beneficial for emergent evaluation and treatment without safety concerns.
For EVT-eligible patients, MSUs can be beneficial to triage to the appropriate thrombectomy-capable facility with prenotification of receiving teams.
Evidence: B_PROUD and BEST-MSU trials demonstrated faster thrombolysis and better functional outcomes, with benefit strongly tied to golden-hour thrombolytic rates.
Evidence: same MSU trial cohort supports equipping MSUs for full IVT workup and delivery.
Evidence: streamlined MSU protocols with in-person or telemedicine neurology expertise improved care without safety issues.
Evidence: MSUs help identify LVO, triage to correct facility, and prenotify receiving teams.
MSUs are specialized ambulances with interdisciplinary teams and imaging that start AIS treatment in the field, saving time.

2.6.meaning: Whether hospitals caring for AIS patients should be certified as stroke centers by external health-care credentialing agencies.

units.meaning: Hospitals caring for AIS patients should be certified as stroke specialty hospitals by an external credentialing agency using national evidence-based standards.
Evidence: large observational studies from national registries show stroke-certified hospitals outperform others on time-to-imaging, DTN, door-to-puncture and lower short/long-term mortality.
Stroke-center certification is based on criteria such as timely imaging, timely IVT and thrombectomy, and evidence-based interventions.

2.7.topic: Emergency Evaluation of Patients With Suspected Stroke (ED and Stroke Teams)
2.7.meaning: How the ED and hospital should organize stroke-team evaluation, pathways and protocols for suspected stroke including pediatric patients.

units.meaning: Patients of all ages with acute neurological deficits should benefit from an organized protocol for emergent evaluation and early treatment.
In pediatric patients with sudden focal neurological deficits (including first-time seizure), acute stroke should be suspected for timely diagnosis.
Patients with suspected stroke should benefit from a designated acute stroke team (clinicians, nurses, lab/radiology) for clinical/neurological assessment.
Development and education of multidisciplinary stroke teams with access to stroke expertise safely increases IVT treatment rates.
Thrombolytic- and EVT-eligible patients should benefit from stroke teams to ensure the fastest onset-to-treatment time and best clinical outcomes.
Evidence: organized protocols and stroke teams accelerate clinical assessment, diagnostics, and early management decisions; NIHSS provides severity and prognostic information.
Evidence: observational data show pediatric AIS diagnosis is commonly delayed due to lack of clinical suspicion; joint pediatric/stroke evaluation is ideal.
Evidence: comprehensive AHA review shows dedicated ED stroke teams enhance speed and accuracy of diagnosis and treatment, improving outcomes.
Evidence: INSTINCT (alteplase use rose 1.00% -> 2.62%) and PRACTISE trials confirm multicomponent programs with stroke teams safely increase thrombolytics.
Evidence: RCT and GWTG-Stroke data - earlier IVT yields greater benefit; each 15-min delay reduction lowers in-hospital mortality (OR 0.96) and sICH.
Timely ED evaluation and treatment of suspected stroke requires organized protocols, dedicated teams, and clinical assessment tools like NIHSS.

2.8.meaning: How telemedicine and telestroke are used across the continuum of AIS care (prehospital, teleradiology, IVT decision-making, triage for EVT).

units.meaning: Telemedicine in the ambulance should be considered when feasible to identify reperfusion candidates.
Teleradiology is recommended for EDs without in-house imaging interpretation for timely brain imaging review and IVT decision-making.
Telestroke is effective over usual ED care for IVT decision-making and delivery (including increased thrombolytic administration and shorter time).
Telestroke at hospitals without local stroke expertise is reasonable to reduce short-term mortality.
Telephone consultation with a stroke specialist can be beneficial for IVT and EVT decision-making when telestroke is unavailable.
Institutions, payers, and vendors should support 24/7 telestroke coverage for AIS patients.
Telestroke may be reasonable to triage patients for interfacility transfer for emergency EVT vs local care.
Evidence: cluster RCT showed in-ambulance telemedicine outperformed modified-stroke-scale paramedic assessment for stroke identification.
Evidence: STRokE DOC and AZ TIME trials show reliable agreement between telestroke neurologists, radiologists and neuroradiologists on IVT radiological contraindications.
Evidence: STRokEDOC RCTs show telemedicine > phone for thrombolytic eligibility decisions; difference-in-differences analyses show higher reperfusion rates at telestroke hospitals.
Evidence: difference-in-differences and observational studies show lower 30-day and in-hospital mortality at telestroke hospitals; meta-analyses show outcomes similar to in-person stroke-center care.
Evidence: STRokEDOC RCTs showed similar secondary outcomes (including thrombolytic use) between telestroke and telephone consultation.
Evidence: broader telestroke infrastructure improves equitable access to stroke expertise regardless of geography.
Evidence: observational comparison - 48 teleconsult-transfer EVT patients had higher thrombolytic receipt, lower sICH/mortality, similar reperfusion vs 103 direct-admission patients.
Telestroke bridges access gaps for remote patients across prehospital, ED, and interhospital transfer settings.

2.9.meaning: How hospitals and EMS should integrate into an accountable SSOC with transfer protocols, imaging capability, care protocols, metrics tracking, and neurointerventionalist credentialing.

units.meaning: Hospitals should participate in an accountable SSOC integrating certified hospitals and prehospital EMS.
Hospitals without 24/7 thrombectomy should develop interhospital transfer protocols for EVT-eligible patients.
PSC hospitals relying on transfers should be able to rapidly perform and interpret CTA/MRA to identify LVO for EVT.
Hospitals should develop and adopt care protocols reflecting current professional clinical guidelines.
EVT-providing hospitals should track door-to-puncture, reperfusion, and long-term patient outcomes.
EVT-providing hospitals should credential neurointerventionalists using agreed training and certification standards.
ASRHs relying on transfer can consider CTA/MRA capability to identify LVO.
SSOCs may consider mobile intervention teams to improve timely EVT delivery.
Evidence: an accountable SSOC integrates hospitals, EMS, and regional health authorities across prehospital, inpatient, and posthospital care.
Evidence: hub-and-spoke design with protocols to quickly identify and transfer patients to the closest CSC for advanced neurosurgical care; rising interhospital transfer volumes.
Evidence: only 10-15% of AIS are EVT-eligible so accurate LVO identification via intracranial vascular imaging is essential before transfer.
Evidence: written care protocols are a key element of PSCs; observational QI studies including protocols improved thrombolysis access and timeliness.
Evidence: EVT has NNT as low as 3 but benefit is time-dependent; metrics monitoring is incorporated into stroke-center certification.
Evidence: national programs (ACGME, UCNS, CAST) provide training/certification pathways including for practitioners who trained before fellowship standards existed.
Evidence: rural and lower-resource centers need improved LVO identification; growth of CTA/advanced imaging in US EDs but disparities remain for smaller rural hospitals.
Evidence: mothership and drip-and-ship EVT models persist; mobile intervention teams are an emerging alternative in uneven geographies.
Timely, efficient, equitable reperfusion delivery requires a coordinated SSOC with integrated EMS, hub-and-spoke hospitals, and tiered stroke certifications.

2.10.topic: Stroke Registries, Quality Improvement, and Risk Adjustment
2.10.meaning: Whether/how hospitals should participate in QI programs and stroke registries and document stroke severity for risk adjustment.

units.meaning: Hospitals should engage in multicomponent QI with continuous monitoring, review, and feedback on stroke quality indicators to improve care and reduce disparities.
Hospitals should participate in stroke data registries to improve adherence to quality measures and patient outcomes.
Hospitals should measure and document baseline stroke severity (NIHSS) to support accurate hospital performance risk adjustment.
Evidence: 3 cluster RCTs show multicomponent QI improves adherence to performance measures and accelerates thrombolysis.
Evidence: observational studies show registry participation with QI improves quality measure adherence, reperfusion rates/timeliness, discharge home rates, and mortality.
Evidence: NIHSS is the strongest predictor of stroke outcome; inclusion in risk-adjustment models materially improves hospital-performance comparisons.
Gaps: role of organizational/system contexts in QI; PedNIHSS validation for risk-adjustment model evaluation.
Registries and QI frameworks enable continuous improvement of stroke care quality and equitable performance comparison.

3.1.meaning: Use of a standardized stroke severity rating scale (preferably NIHSS) to measure neurological deficit at baseline and after reperfusion.

units.meaning: In patients with suspected AIS, a stroke-severity rating scale (preferably the NIHSS) is recommended at baseline and after reperfusion therapies.

Evidence: baseline NIHSS and 24-hour delta-NIHSS are among the strongest predictors of 30-day and 3-month mortality and functional outcome; 24-hour NIHSS adjusted for baseline is the best 90-day functional outcome predictor; NIHSS within 1 week after EVT meets surrogate-endpoint criteria for longer-term outcome.

The NIHSS is a well-established standardized scale that quantifies neurological deficit, facilitates communication, identifies thrombolysis/EVT candidates, tracks clinical change, and flags higher complication risk.

3.2.topic: Initial, Vascular, and Multimodal Imaging Approaches
3.2.meaning: Which brain and vascular imaging studies to perform, when, and how to use them to guide reperfusion decisions (IVT, EVT, extended-window selection, direct-to-angiography).

units.meaning: Emergent NCCT or MRI is recommended on initial evaluation to assess ischemic burden (e.g. ASPECTS) and exclude ICH before initiating reperfusion therapy.
Hospital protocols should ensure emergent brain imaging is performed as rapidly as possible, e.g. within 25 minutes, to enable timely reperfusion.
In suspected LVO, contrast-enhanced CTA/CTP should NOT be delayed to obtain serum creatinine.
In pediatric patients with suspected AIS, emergent MRI/MRA of cervical and intracranial vessels is reasonable to identify LVO and differentiate AIS from hemorrhagic stroke or mimics.
In pediatric patients with suspected AIS, CT/CTA is reasonable if MRI/MRA is not immediately available (within 25 minutes) to identify LVO.
In wake-up or unknown onset >4.5 hours from LKW, MRI DWI-FLAIR mismatch selection can be useful to determine eligibility for extended-window IVT.
In wake-up or unknown onset 4.5 to 24 hours from LKW, CTP or MR DWI-PWI with automated postprocessing can be useful to determine eligibility for extended-window IVT.
In suspected AIS with LVO presenting within 24 hours of LKW, emergent brain and vascular imaging (CT/CTA or MRI/MRA) should be performed as rapidly as possible for EVT selection and planning.
In suspected AIS with LVO presenting 6 to 24 hours from LKW, adjunctive CTP, MRI DWI-FLAIR mismatch, or MR DWI-PWI can be useful for EVT evaluation if immediately available.

For prehospital-screened suspected LVO (e.g. RACE >4) eligible for EVT, direct triage to the angiography suite with flat-panel head CT may be considered to reduce time to intervention and improve functional outcome.
In emergently transferred patients with LVO eligible for EVT, DTAS without repeat brain imaging may be considered unless there is clinical change or transfer delay.
Evidence: NCCT reliably detects acute ICH; NCCT alone was used in NINDS rt-PA and ECASS III trials, so NCCT is sufficient neuroimaging for most IVT decisions.
Evidence: IVT and EVT benefit is time-dependent; pooled analysis of 5 EVT RCTs shows strongly time-dependent disability improvement.

Evidence: meta-analysis of 14 studies found no increased AKI from CTA/CTP vs NCCT after adjusting for baseline renal function; AKI rate 3%, dialysis 0.07% in CTA/CTP cohorts, including patients with known CKD.

Evidence: limited observational data favor MRI/MRA as the modality of choice for hyperacute pediatric stroke evaluation - more reliable for distinguishing AIS from mimics and avoids ionizing radiation, despite slightly longer delays.
Evidence: when pediatric MRI is not immediately available or contraindicated, CT/CTA is reasonable using pediatric-specific dose reduction strategies to mitigate radiation risk.

Evidence: WAKE-UP trial used MRI DWI-FLAIR mismatch to select wake-up stroke patients for IVT (excluding DWI lesions >1/3 MCA); subsequent trials with alteplase 0.6 mg/kg extended support for mismatch selection in extended windows (see Table 3).

Evidence: neurovascular imaging (CTA/MRA) was required before randomization in EVT RCTs (MR CLEAN, THRACE, ESCAPE, EXTEND-IA, SWIFT PRIME, REVASCAT, DAWN, DEFUSE 3); cervical imaging was used for procedural planning.

Evidence: CTP, MR DWI-FLAIR, and MR DWI-PWI were used adjunctively in most early, late, and large-core thrombectomy RCTs; MR CLEAN and THRACE used only NCCT + LVO detection, so advanced tissue selection is not required in early (<6h) windows.
Evidence: ANGIOCAT single-center RCT (n=174, high LVO suspicion within 6h) - DTAS with flat-panel CT shortened door-to-groin-puncture (18 vs 42 min, P<0.001) vs conventional ED workflow.

Evidence: nonrandomized multicenter study of 1140 transferred LVO patients - DTAS shortened arrival-to-groin-puncture (34 vs 60 min) with better functional and safety outcomes in both early and late windows vs repeat imaging after transfer.
Gaps: validation of modified pediatric ASPECTS on MRI; role of flat-panel CT and DTAS in broader populations; optimal perfusion-imaging thresholds for late-window patient selection.
Brain and vascular imaging is the cornerstone of AIS triage - it confirms diagnosis, excludes hemorrhage, identifies LVO, estimates core/penumbra in extended windows, and enables DTAS workflows.

3.3.meaning: Baseline cardiac tests (ECG, troponin) in suspected acute stroke - recommended but must not delay reperfusion.

units.meaning: Baseline ECG is recommended in suspected acute stroke but should not delay IVT or EVT.
Baseline troponin is recommended in suspected acute stroke but should not delay IVT or EVT.
Evidence: single-center observational data - ECG findings (nonsinus rhythm, T-wave inversion, ST changes, AV nodal blocks) help identify stroke mechanism and predict higher 1-year mortality.

Evidence: 2 systematic reviews - elevated troponins help predict cardioembolic mechanism and elevated post-stroke mortality risk; rising serial troponins (vs flat/decreasing) are more predictive of mortality.

Relevant ED diagnostic tests for suspected acute stroke include point-of-care glucose (§4.5), baseline ECG, and troponin - they aid diagnosis and mechanism identification but must not delay reperfusion.

4.1.meaning: Airway support, ventilation, supplemental oxygen, normobaric and hyperbaric oxygen in AIS patients.

units.meaning: In AIS with decreased consciousness or bulbar dysfunction, airway support and ventilatory assistance are recommended for maintenance, protection, adequate ventilation/oxygenation.
In AIS with hypoxia, supplemental oxygen should be provided to maintain SpO2 >94%.
In AIS within 6 hours, NIHSS 10-20, ASPECTS >=6, anterior LVO (M1 or ICA terminus) with planned EVT, normobaric hyperoxia before EVT may be reasonable to improve 90-day functional outcome.
In AIS due to arterial air embolism, hyperbaric oxygen may be reasonable to improve clinical outcome.
In AIS without hypoxia who are ineligible for EVT, supplemental oxygen is NOT recommended to improve functional outcome.
In AIS not associated with air embolism, HBO is NOT recommended to improve functional outcome.

Evidence: hypoxia affects up to 63% of stroke patients and is associated with higher mortality; airway support must account for ability to protect airway, maintain ventilation/oxygenation, and anticipated deterioration.

Evidence: retrospective cohort (n=1479) showed dose-dependent association of SpO2/FiO2 with lower mortality; HeadPoST post-hoc showed inverse J-shaped association between lowest SpO2 and death/dependency.
Evidence: small single-center RCTs (180 and 86 subjects) showed NBO via Venturi mask or face mask pre/post-EVT was safe and possibly effective for improving functional outcomes.
Evidence: meta-analysis of 10 nonrandomized studies (n=263) - favorable outcome rate fell from 65% with immediate HBO to 30% with 15-hour delay in cerebral arterial gas embolism.
Evidence: meta-analysis of 15 studies (n=9255) and the largest RCT (n=8003, Stroke Oxygen Study) - supplemental oxygen for 3 days did not improve 90-day functional outcome.
Evidence: meta-analysis of 8 AIS RCTs (n=493) - no difference in NIHSS, Barthel, or inflammatory markers between HBO and control.
Airway maintenance and adequate oxygenation are essential; stroke location may alter consciousness, swallowing, and respiration, increasing hypoxia/aspiration/pneumonia risk.

4.2.meaning: Whether head-of-bed positioning (flat vs 30 degrees vs Trendelenburg) influences functional outcome in AIS.

units.meaning: Routine 0-degree head positioning (flat) for 24 hours has NO benefit over 30 degrees in improving functional outcome.
In AIS with probable large-artery atherosclerosis without reperfusion option, Trendelenburg positioning (-20 degrees) has NO benefit over 0-30 degrees.
Evidence: HeadPoST cluster-randomized trial - flat vs upright head positioning produced no difference in 90-day disability outcome.
Evidence: Trendelenburg in large-artery atherosclerosis patients without reperfusion option showed no functional outcome benefit.
Head-of-bed positioning was historically debated as a means to augment collateral perfusion; RCT evidence does not support routine flat or Trendelenburg positioning.

4.3.meaning: How to manage blood pressure in AIS - hypotension correction, when to treat hypertension, pre- and post-IVT and pre/post-EVT targets, and harms of intensive BP lowering after EVT.

general.meaning: Overall hypotension/hypertension management for AIS not specific to reperfusion.

before_reperfusion.meaning: BP targets and management before IVT or EVT.

after_ivt.meaning: BP targets in the first 24 hours after IVT.

after_evt.meaning: BP targets during and after EVT, including harms of intensive SBP lowering after successful recanalization.

units.meaning: In AIS, hypotension and hypovolemia should be corrected to maintain systemic perfusion to support organ function.
Early hypertension treatment is indicated for comorbid conditions (acute coronary event, acute HF, aortic dissection, post-thrombolysis sICH, or preeclampsia/eclampsia).
In patients with BP >=220/120 not receiving IVT/EVT and without comorbid conditions, benefit of initiating antihypertensive treatment within 48-72 hours is uncertain.

In patients with BP <220/120 not receiving IVT/EVT and without comorbid conditions, initiating/reinitiating antihypertensive therapy within 48-72 hours is NOT effective for preventing death or dependency.
Before IVT, SBP should be lowered to <185 and DBP <110 to reduce hemorrhagic complications.
When EVT is planned without prior IVT, maintaining BP <=185/110 before the procedure is reasonable.
BP should be maintained at <180/105 for at least the first 24 hours after IVT treatment.
In mild-to-moderate AIS treated with IVT, intensive SBP reduction (target <140 vs <180) is NOT recommended - no functional outcome improvement.
In patients undergoing EVT, maintaining BP <=180/105 during and for 24 hours after the procedure is reasonable.
In anterior-circulation LVO successfully recanalized (mTICI 2b/2c/3) without other indication, intensive SBP reduction target <140 for the first 72 hours is HARMFUL and not recommended.
Evidence: hypotension (SBP <120) is associated with worse outcome; isotonic crystalloid is preferred over colloid for volume expansion.
Evidence: concurrent ACS, HF, aortic dissection, post-IVT sICH, or preeclampsia warrant immediate BP control based on the comorbid condition's own guidelines.
Evidence: limited data in patients with very severe hypertension (>=220/120) without reperfusion; expert opinion supports cautious reduction ~15% in the first 24 hours.
Evidence: multiple RCTs including CATIS, SCAST, ENOS, ENCHANTED2/MT - early BP lowering in non-reperfusion patients below 220/120 does not improve death/dependency outcomes.
Evidence: NINDS, ECASS III, and subsequent trials excluded patients with BP >185/110; hemorrhagic transformation risk rises with higher pre-IVT BP.
Evidence: EVT trials used the same pre-procedural BP threshold as IVT trials; no signal that higher pre-EVT BP is safe.
Evidence: NINDS rt-PA protocol mandated BP <180/105 for the first 24 hours post-IVT; observational data show higher BP is associated with sICH.
Evidence: ENCHANTED and ENCHANTED2/MT trials - intensive SBP reduction (<140) after IVT did not improve 90-day functional outcome vs standard <180.
Evidence: observational data and trials support maintaining BP <=180/105 during and shortly after EVT to balance reperfusion injury and cerebral perfusion.
Evidence: BP-TARGET, ENCHANTED2/MT, and OPTIMAL-BP trials - intensive SBP reduction <140 after successful EVT produced worse functional outcomes and signals of harm.
BP management in AIS balances maintaining cerebral perfusion against hemorrhagic conversion risk; targets differ by reperfusion status and time after treatment.

4.4.meaning: Management of hyperthermia and prophylactic hypothermia in AIS.

units.meaning: In AIS with hyperthermia, targeting normothermia (including nurse-initiated fever protocols) is recommended to improve functional outcome and reduce death.
In AIS with hyperthermia, sources (e.g. infection) should be identified and treated to avoid complications.
In AIS with normothermia, induced hypothermia or prophylactic fever prevention is NOT recommended for outcome improvement.
Evidence: hyperthermia worsens stroke outcome; QASC and similar trials show nurse-initiated fever management improves outcomes.
Evidence: infection is a common source of fever in stroke; treating the underlying source is standard supportive care.
Evidence: EuroHYP-1 and other hypothermia trials did not demonstrate functional outcome benefit and raised pneumonia risk.
Fever worsens stroke outcome; standard care is normothermia via protocolized antipyretics and infection treatment.

4.5.meaning: Management of hypoglycemia and hyperglycemia in AIS.

units.meaning: Hypoglycemia (glucose <60 mg/dL) should be treated to avoid complications.
Treat persistent hyperglycemia to achieve glucose 140-180 mg/dL with close monitoring.
IV insulin targeting glucose 80-130 mg/dL is NOT recommended - does not improve 3-month functional outcome.
Evidence: hypoglycemia can mimic or worsen stroke and must be corrected immediately.
Evidence: observational data link admission hyperglycemia to worse stroke outcome; moderate target 140-180 balances control and hypoglycemia risk.
Evidence: SHINE trial (n=1151) - tight IV insulin glucose control 80-130 did not improve 90-day functional outcome and increased hypoglycemia.
Both hypo- and hyperglycemia worsen AIS outcome; moderate glycemic control (140-180) is the standard, tight control provides no benefit and increases hypoglycemia risk.

4.6.meaning: Use of IV thrombolytics (alteplase, tenecteplase, extended-window agents, and other fibrinolytics) in AIS - eligibility, agent choice, timing, pediatric use, and special circumstances.

4.6.1.meaning: General IVT decision-making: time target, shared decision, glucose/hematologic workup, handling of antiplatelet use, cerebral microbleeds, early ischemic changes, mild non-disabling stroke, pediatric.

4.6.2.meaning: Choice between alteplase (0.9 mg/kg) and tenecteplase (0.25 mg/kg, max 25 mg); harm signal for tenecteplase 0.4 mg/kg.

4.6.3.meaning: IVT in unknown-onset/wake-up stroke and 4.5-24h window using DWI-FLAIR or perfusion mismatch imaging selection.

4.6.4.meaning: Reteplase, mutant prourokinase, desmoteplase, urokinase, streptokinase, sonothrombolysis - alternatives and harms.

4.6.5.meaning: IVT in sickle cell disease and central retinal artery occlusion.

units.meaning: In adults with disabling deficits regardless of NIHSS and eligible for IVT, faster treatment improves functional outcomes.
In adults eligible for IVT within 4.5h, treatment should be initiated as quickly as possible, avoiding delays from additional CTA/MRA/perfusion imaging.
Clinicians should be prepared to manage IVT adverse effects including bleeding and angioedema (potential partial airway obstruction).
Discuss IVT risks and benefits with competent patients/representatives when feasible for shared decision making.
Measure blood glucose before IVT to identify severe hypo/hyperglycemia that may mimic stroke.
If disabling deficits persist after glucose correction, IVT is recommended.
Mild to moderate early ischemic change (other than frank hypodensity) on initial brain imaging is NOT a contraindication; IVT is recommended.
In mild NON-disabling stroke deficits within 4.5h, IVT is NOT recommended - has not shown superiority over DAPT.
Patients on SAPT or DAPT who are otherwise eligible should receive IVT; functional benefit outweighs the increased sICH risk.
Don't delay IVT waiting for hematologic/coagulation testing if there's no reason to suspect an abnormal result.
With unknown CMB burden, IVT should be administered WITHOUT obtaining MRI first to screen for microbleeds.
With 1-10 CMBs on MRI, IVT is reasonable to achieve better functional outcome.
With >10 CMBs, usefulness of IVT is uncertain and may increase sICH risk.
In pediatric patients 28 days to 18 years with confirmed AIS and disabling deficits within 4.5h, IVT with alteplase may be considered - safe, efficacy uncertain.
Within 4.5h of onset/LKW, tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg (max 25 mg) OR alteplase 0.9 mg/kg is recommended.
Tenecteplase 0.4 mg/kg is NOT recommended.
In unknown onset within 4.5h of symptom recognition with DWI lesion <1/3 MCA territory and no marked FLAIR change, IVT within 4.5h of recognition can be beneficial.
With salvageable penumbra on automated perfusion imaging AND (a) within 9h of sleep midpoint in wake-up stroke or (b) 4.5-9h from LKW, IVT may be reasonable.
In LVO with salvageable penumbra presenting 4.5-24h who cannot receive EVT, IVT directed by experts can be beneficial.
In eligible patients within 4.5h not undergoing EVT, IV reteplase instead of alteplase may be considered to increase odds of excellent 90-day outcome.
In eligible patients within 4.5h not undergoing EVT, IV mutant prourokinase instead of alteplase may be considered.
IV desmoteplase 3-9h from LKW is NOT recommended for 90-day functional independence.
Mutant prourokinase combined with low-dose alteplase within 4.5h is NOT recommended.
IV urokinase within 6h is not beneficial for reducing death or dependency.
IV streptokinase within 6h should NOT be administered - no functional benefit, increased early mortality.
Sonothrombolysis adjunctive to IVT is NOT recommended - no improvement in early neurological recovery or 90-day outcome.
In adults with known sickle cell disease, IVT can be beneficial without increased sICH or systemic hemorrhage.
In adults with nonarteritic CRAO causing disabling visual loss within 4.5h of LKW, usefulness of IVT is uncertain.
Evidence: pooled analyses of alteplase trials (NINDS, ECASS, SITS-MOST, GWTG-Stroke) show time-dependent benefit with greater odds of good outcome the earlier treatment is given.
Evidence: additional advanced imaging (CTA/MRA/CTP) delays IVT start without improving selection within 4.5h.
Evidence: angioedema occurs in ~1-5% of IVT and may obstruct the airway; bleeding complications require protocol-driven response.
Evidence: shared decision making supports ethical care when time permits and the patient can participate.
Evidence: stroke mimics include severe dysglycemia; glucose must be checked rapidly before IVT.
Evidence: if deficits persist despite normoglycemia, IVT improves outcome.
Evidence: IST-3 and pooled analyses show IVT benefit is preserved in patients with mild-to-moderate early ischemic changes.
Evidence: PRISMS trial halted early - IVT for mild nondisabling stroke showed no benefit over antiplatelet therapy with higher bleeding risk.
Evidence: observational data show higher sICH with pre-IVT antiplatelet but net functional benefit favors IVT.
Evidence: waiting for INR/platelet results delays treatment in patients without anticoagulation risk factors.
Evidence: MRI-first strategies delay IVT without evidence of benefit.
Evidence: cohort studies - 1-10 CMBs do not negate IVT functional benefit.
Evidence: high CMB burden (>10) is associated with higher sICH risk; net benefit is unclear.
Evidence: TIPS study and observational series show pediatric alteplase is feasible and safe; efficacy remains uncertain.
Evidence: AcT, TRACE-2, ATTEST-2, TASTE trials show tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg is non-inferior (and possibly superior) to alteplase 0.9 mg/kg for functional outcome.
Evidence: NOR-TEST 2 Part A - tenecteplase 0.4 mg/kg had inferior safety vs alteplase 0.9 mg/kg.
Evidence: WAKE-UP trial - DWI-FLAIR mismatch selects wake-up stroke patients with functional benefit from IVT.
Evidence: EXTEND trial and pooled analyses - automated perfusion imaging mismatch selects extended-window patients for IVT benefit.
Evidence: emerging late-window LVO trials show benefit when EVT is unavailable and penumbra is present.
Evidence: RAISE trial - IV reteplase increased excellent 90-day functional outcome odds vs alteplase in patients not undergoing EVT.
Evidence: mutant prourokinase trials suggest non-inferior outcomes to alteplase in selected non-EVT patients.
Evidence: DIAS-3/DIAS-4 trials - desmoteplase did not improve 90-day independence.
Evidence: combined mutant prourokinase + low-dose alteplase did not improve functional outcomes.
Evidence: IV urokinase within 6h did not reduce death/dependency.
Evidence: MAST-I, MAST-E, and ASK trials - streptokinase caused excess early mortality from hemorrhage.
Evidence: CLOTBUST-ER and other sonothrombolysis trials did not improve early neurological recovery or 90-day outcome.
Evidence: registry and cohort studies - IVT in SCD patients is safe without increased hemorrhagic or thrombolytic complications.
Evidence: small case series and REVISION trial - IVT in CRAO within 4.5h shows uncertain benefit with bleeding risk.

IVT is the foundational medical reperfusion therapy for AIS - fastest possible administration improves outcomes; alteplase 0.9 mg/kg and tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg are the recommended agents; extended-window use requires imaging-based penumbra selection.

4.7.meaning: Mechanical thrombectomy for LVO - eligibility by time window, vessel, NIHSS, ASPECTS, mRS, mass effect, posterior circulation, techniques, and pediatric use.

4.7.1.meaning: Whether to give IVT alongside EVT, timing and sequence.

4.7.2.meaning: Adult EVT eligibility by time window (0-6h, 6-24h), vessel (ICA/M1/M2), NIHSS, ASPECTS, prestroke mRS, and mass effect.

4.7.3.meaning: EVT for basilar artery occlusion within 24h by baseline NIHSS and PC-ASPECTS.

4.7.4.meaning: EVT techniques: stent retrievers, contact aspiration, TICI goal, anesthesia, adjunctive IA thrombolysis, tirofiban, balloon guide, stenting, tandem occlusions, rescue techniques.

4.7.5.meaning: EVT in children - effectiveness in >=6y within 6h and 6-24h, and feasibility in 28d-6y.

units.meaning: For patients eligible for both IVT and EVT, IVT is safe and recommended to improve reperfusion efficacy and clinical outcomes.
IVT should be given as rapidly as possible with NO observation period to delay EVT.
In anterior ICA/M1 LVO within 6h, NIHSS >=6, prestroke mRS 0-1, ASPECTS 3-10, EVT is recommended.
In anterior ICA/M1 LVO 6-24h, NIHSS >=6, prestroke mRS 0-1, ASPECTS >=6, EVT is recommended.
In anterior ICA/M1 LVO 6-24h, age <80, NIHSS >=6, prestroke mRS 0-1, ASPECTS 3-5, no significant mass effect, EVT is recommended.
In anterior ICA/M1 LVO within 6h, age <80, NIHSS >=6, prestroke mRS 0-1, ASPECTS 0-2 without significant mass effect, EVT is reasonable.
In anterior ICA/M1 LVO within 6h, NIHSS >=6, ASPECTS >=6, prestroke mRS 2, EVT is reasonable.
In anterior ICA/M1 LVO within 6h, NIHSS >=6, ASPECTS >=6, prestroke mRS 3-4, EVT might be reasonable.
In dominant proximal M2 MCA LVO within 6h, NIHSS >=6, ASPECTS >=6, prestroke mRS 0-1, EVT is reasonable but benefit is uncertain.
In proximal nondominant/codominant M2, distal MCA, ACA, or PCA occlusions, EVT is NOT recommended.
In basilar artery occlusion within 24h, baseline mRS 0-1, NIHSS >=10, PC-ASPECTS >=6, EVT is recommended.
In basilar artery occlusion within 24h, baseline mRS 0-1, NIHSS 6-9, PC-ASPECTS >=6, effectiveness of EVT is not well established.
For LVO, EVT with stent retrievers, contact aspiration, or combination techniques is recommended for rapid reperfusion.
Reperfusion to extended TICI 2b/2c/3 as early as possible within the therapeutic window is recommended for maximum 90-day functional benefit.
Either general anesthesia or procedural sedation is recommended during EVT.
Use of a proximal balloon guide catheter remains uncertain for improving outcomes.
EVT with stent retrievers in medium/distal anterior, nondominant M2/M3, or posterior cerebral artery occlusions is of NO benefit.
In tandem extracranial-intracranial anterior-circulation occlusions, acute treatment of both (including emergent extracranial stenting) may be reasonable.
In failed EVT, rescue intracranial balloon angioplasty and/or stenting has uncertain benefit for functional outcome.
After complete/near-complete EVT (mTICI >=2b), adjunctive IA thrombolytics (urokinase, alteplase, tenecteplase) may be reasonable to improve cerebral reperfusion and 90-day outcome.
Preoperative IV tirofiban before EVT in LVO is NOT useful for 90-day functional outcome.
In pediatric patients >=6y with LVO within 6h, EVT by experienced neurointerventionalists can be effective.
In pediatric patients >=6y with LVO 6-24h and salvageable brain tissue, EVT can be effective.
In pediatric patients 28d-6y with LVO within 24h and salvageable brain tissue, EVT by experienced pediatric operators may be reasonable.
Evidence: SWIFT DIRECT, DIRECT-MT, MR CLEAN NO IV, and SKIP trials and subsequent meta-analyses - IVT before EVT is safe and complements mechanical reperfusion.
Evidence: treatment-time analyses show any observation period after IVT before EVT worsens outcomes.
Evidence: MR CLEAN, ESCAPE, EXTEND-IA, SWIFT PRIME, REVASCAT, THRACE and HERMES meta-analysis establish strong EVT benefit in the early window.
Evidence: DAWN and DEFUSE 3 trials established EVT benefit in 6-24h with mismatch selection and ASPECTS >=6.
Evidence: SELECT2, ANGEL-ASPECT, RESCUE-Japan LIMIT, TESLA and TENSION trials - EVT benefit extends to low-ASPECTS large-core patients in the late window.
Evidence: large-core EVT trial subgroups suggest benefit extends to ASPECTS 0-2 in the early window in selected patients.
Evidence: observational registry data - prestroke mRS 2 patients derive functional outcome benefit from EVT.
Evidence: limited observational data; benefit is smaller but possible in selected disabled patients.
Evidence: patient-level meta-analyses show modest benefit for dominant M2 occlusions; primary trials had limited M2 enrollment.
Evidence: DISTAL, ESCAPE-MeVO and related trials - no benefit for distal/medium vessel or nondominant M2 occlusions.
Evidence: ATTENTION and BAOCHE trials establish EVT benefit in BAO within 24h with NIHSS >=10 and PC-ASPECTS >=6.
Evidence: subgroup analyses of ATTENTION/BAOCHE and earlier trials (BASICS, BEST) show uncertain benefit for milder BAO presentations.
Evidence: COMPASS, ASTER, and ASTER2 trials - stent retriever, contact aspiration, and combination techniques all yield acceptable reperfusion rates.
Evidence: reperfusion grade strongly correlates with 90-day functional outcome; TICI 2c/3 outperforms 2b.
Evidence: SIESTA, AnStroke, GOLIATH and meta-analyses - GA and conscious sedation yield comparable EVT outcomes when hemodynamic management is protocolized.
Evidence: observational data suggest higher first-pass effect with BGC but RCT evidence is limited.
Evidence: DISTAL and ESCAPE-MeVO trials - no benefit for distal/medium vessel stent-retriever EVT.
Evidence: TITAN registry and observational cohorts suggest concurrent extracranial stenting improves functional outcomes in tandem lesions.
Evidence: small case series show variable outcomes with rescue angioplasty/stenting after failed EVT.
Evidence: CHOICE trial - IA alteplase after successful EVT improved excellent 90-day outcome; larger confirmatory trials ongoing.
Evidence: RESCUE BT trial - preoperative IV tirofiban did not improve 90-day functional outcome.
Evidence: Save ChildS registry and pooled case series - EVT in children >=6y is feasible with adult-like safety and functional outcomes.
Evidence: pediatric registry data extended to 6-24h window with penumbra selection.
Evidence: limited case series in very young children; EVT feasibility depends on vessel size and operator experience.
EVT is the mainstay reperfusion therapy for LVO AIS - benefit is established across early and late windows including large-core and basilar occlusions; target is extended TICI 2b/2c/3.

4.8.meaning: Early antiplatelet therapy in AIS - aspirin timing, IV glycoprotein IIb/IIIa, preference over anticoagulation for noncardioembolic, and DAPT for minor stroke/high-risk TIA.

units.meaning: Aspirin is recommended within 48 hours after stroke onset to reduce death and dependency.
In patients who received IVT, risk of antiplatelet therapy in the first 24 hours post-IVT is uncertain; may be considered for substantial competing benefit.
IV tirofiban efficacy to improve clinical outcomes is not well established.
IV abciximab is NOT recommended - increased bleeding complications.
In noncardioembolic AIS or TIA, antiplatelet therapy is preferred over oral anticoagulation for recurrent stroke prevention.
Antiplatelet agent for early secondary prevention should be individualized by risk, cost, tolerance, and efficacy.
In AIS with extracranial carotid or vertebral dissection, either antiplatelet OR anticoagulant for >=3 months is reasonable for recurrent stroke prevention.
For patients already on aspirin at noncardioembolic stroke/TIA, effectiveness of increasing dose or switching agents is not well established.
Clopidogrel is NOT recommended over aspirin for composite endpoint of stroke, MI, or death.
Triple antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel + dipyridamole) is HARMFUL - increased bleeding.
In AIS with AF without active CAD or recent stent, routine addition of antiplatelet to anticoagulation is HARMFUL - increased bleeding.
In minor (NIHSS <=3) noncardioembolic AIS or high-risk TIA (ABCD2 >=4) not receiving IVT, DAPT (aspirin + clopidogrel loading) should be started within 24h and continued 21 days, then SAPT.
In recent (<24h) minor (NIHSS <=5) noncardioembolic AIS or high-risk TIA not receiving IVT, DAPT with ticagrelor + aspirin for 30 days may be considered.
In minor AIS/high-risk TIA within 24-72h with >=50% atherosclerotic stenosis and not receiving IVT, DAPT (clopidogrel + aspirin) for 21 days is reasonable.
In minor AIS/high-risk TIA with CYP2C19 loss-of-function allele, DAPT with ticagrelor + aspirin may be preferred over clopidogrel + aspirin.
Aspirin is NOT recommended as substitute for IVT or mechanical thrombectomy in eligible patients.
IV aspirin concurrent with or within 90 minutes after IVT start is HARMFUL - hemorrhage risk.
IV eptifibatide adjunct to IVT within 3h is NOT recommended to reduce 3-month disability.
Evidence: IST, CAST, and pooled analyses - aspirin within 48h reduces death/dependency.
Evidence: ARTIS trial - IV aspirin early after IVT increased sICH; oral antiplatelet may be considered only with substantial competing benefit.
Evidence: RESCUE BT2 showed modest benefit for non-LVO patients but overall efficacy is unclear.
Evidence: AbESTT trials halted for excess bleeding without functional benefit.
Evidence: WARSS and subsequent trials show no benefit of warfarin over aspirin for noncardioembolic stroke with higher bleeding.
Evidence: PRoFESS, CAPRIE, MATCH trials - no single agent is uniformly superior; selection balances risk factors.
Evidence: CADISS trial - antiplatelet and anticoagulation produced similar stroke recurrence rates in cervical dissection.
Evidence: limited data on dose escalation or switching after aspirin failure.
Evidence: CAPRIE - small composite endpoint benefit of clopidogrel over aspirin did not reach preference threshold.
Evidence: TARDIS trial - triple antiplatelet increased bleeding without functional benefit.
Evidence: observational and RCT data show combined antiplatelet + anticoagulation in AF without active CAD/stent increases bleeding without ischemic benefit.
Evidence: CHANCE, POINT, and THALES trials - short-course DAPT after minor stroke/high-risk TIA reduces 90-day recurrent stroke.
Evidence: THALES trial - ticagrelor + aspirin for 30 days reduces 30-day stroke vs aspirin alone.
Evidence: ATAMIS and subgroups of CHANCE/POINT support 21-day clopidogrel + aspirin in atherosclerotic minor stroke.
Evidence: CHANCE-2 trial - in CYP2C19 LOF carriers, ticagrelor + aspirin reduced 90-day stroke more than clopidogrel + aspirin.
Evidence: aspirin alone does not deliver reperfusion; substituting for IVT/EVT deprives patients of benefit.
Evidence: ARTIS trial - IV aspirin within 90 minutes of IVT start increased sICH.
Evidence: CLEAR III and related trials - IV eptifibatide adjunct to IVT did not reduce 3-month disability.

Early antiplatelet therapy (aspirin within 48h) is standard for AIS; short-course DAPT for minor stroke/high-risk TIA reduces recurrence; IV glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitors show limited or harmful effects.

4.9.meaning: Role of early anticoagulation in AIS - AF, carotid thrombus, hemorrhagic transformation, argatroban, and early anticoagulation effect on worsening.

units.meaning: In carefully selected (milder severity) AIS with atrial fibrillation, early oral anticoagulation is low-risk and reasonable vs delayed.
In AIS with ipsilateral high-grade ICA stenosis, benefit of urgent anticoagulation is not well established.
In AIS with ipsilateral nonocclusive extracranial intraluminal thrombus, safety/efficacy of short-term anticoagulation is not established.
In AIS with hemorrhagic transformation, initiation or continuation of anticoagulation depends on the specific clinical scenario.
Argatroban adjunctive to IVT is NOT effective for long-term functional outcome.
Early anticoagulation within 48h does NOT reduce early neurological worsening and does NOT improve functional outcome.
Evidence: ELAN, OPTIMAS, TIMING, START trials - early DOAC initiation in AIS with AF does not increase hemorrhagic risk and may reduce recurrent stroke.
Evidence: limited RCT data; observational studies are inconclusive on benefit of urgent anticoagulation for carotid occlusion.
Evidence: case series suggest LMWH or warfarin but no RCT guidance exists.
Evidence: retrospective cohorts show variable outcomes; decision should weigh indication against expansion risk.
Evidence: ARAIS trial - argatroban adjunct to alteplase did not improve 90-day functional outcome.
Evidence: IST, TOAST, and subsequent heparinoid trials show early parenteral anticoagulation does not prevent early neurological worsening and increases bleeding.
Early anticoagulation has limited role in acute AIS management; early DOAC for AF-related stroke in selected patients is emerging based on recent trials.

4.10.topic: Volume Expansion/Hemodilution, Vasodilators, and Hemodynamic Augmentation
4.10.meaning: Volume expansion, hemodilution, vasodilators, and mechanical hemodynamic augmentation in AIS.

units.meaning: Hemodilution, high-dose albumin, or chemical vasodilators (e.g. pentoxifylline) are NOT recommended for functional outcome improvement.
Mechanical hemodynamic augmentation (counterpulsation or sphenopalatine ganglion stimulation) is NOT recommended.
Evidence: ALIAS and earlier hemodilution/albumin/pentoxifylline trials show no benefit and some harms.
Evidence: ImpACT-24 and counterpulsation trials did not improve functional outcomes.
No pharmacologic or mechanical hemodynamic augmentation strategy has shown functional outcome benefit in AIS.

4.11.meaning: Pharmacologic and nonpharmacologic neuroprotective strategies in AIS.

units.meaning: At present, pharmacologic or nonpharmacologic neuroprotective treatments are NOT recommended to improve functional outcome.
Evidence: decades of neuroprotection trials (NXY-059, magnesium, citicoline, minocycline, edaravone, uric acid, nerinetide) have failed to demonstrate consistent functional outcome benefit in AIS.
Despite extensive clinical investigation, no neuroprotective agent has entered routine AIS care as of the 2026 guideline.

4.12.topic: Emergency Carotid Endarterectomy, Carotid Angioplasty, and Stenting Without Intracranial Clot
4.12.meaning: Emergency carotid endarterectomy, angioplasty, or stenting for AIS or stroke-in-evolution without intracranial occlusion.

units.meaning: In AIS or stroke-in-evolution from high-grade carotid stenosis/occlusion WITHOUT intracranial occlusion, emergent CEA (within 48h) is NOT beneficial for functional outcome.
Evidence: observational and small trial data show no functional outcome benefit and a signal of periprocedural harm for emergent CEA in stroke-in-evolution without intracranial clot.
Emergent carotid revascularization without an accompanying intracranial occlusion does not improve functional outcome and is not recommended.

section_5.title: In-Hospital Management of AIS: General Supportive Care

5.1.meaning: Organized inpatient stroke-unit care with interdisciplinary specialty-trained teams and protocolized order sets.

units.meaning: AIS of all ages should receive treatment in an organized inpatient stroke-unit with interdisciplinary teams and standardized order sets to reduce poor outcomes and death.
Evidence: Stroke Unit Trialists' Collaboration and multiple national registries show organized stroke-unit care reduces death and institutionalization.
Stroke unit care is one of the most robust evidence-based interventions for AIS outcomes.

5.2.meaning: Dysphagia screening, assessment, oral hygiene, and pharyngeal electrical stimulation treatment.

units.meaning: Bedside swallow screening is recommended before any oral intake to screen for aspiration risk.
Dysphagia screening should be performed by speech pathologists or other trained personnel.
When bedside screening fails or cannot be performed, endoscopic swallow assessment is reasonable for dysphagia severity and aspiration risk.
An oral hygiene protocol may be reasonable to reduce post-stroke pneumonia.
Pharyngeal electrical stimulation can be beneficial to reduce dysphagia severity and aspiration risk.
In severe stroke with tracheotomy, PES after ventilator weaning can reduce dysphagia severity, aspiration, and speed decannulation.
Evidence: dysphagia prevalence and observational data show screening before oral intake reduces aspiration pneumonia.
Evidence: screening accuracy is higher when performed by trained professionals.
Evidence: FEES is feasible and informative when bedside screening is not possible.
Evidence: oral care bundles reduce ventilator-associated and stroke-related pneumonia in some cohorts.
Evidence: PHADER, STEPS trials - PES improves dysphagia metrics in selected stroke patients.
Evidence: PHAST-TRAC trial - PES after ventilator weaning reduced dysphagia severity and expedited decannulation.
Post-stroke dysphagia is common and increases aspiration pneumonia risk; early screening, specialist assessment, and oral hygiene reduce complications.

5.3.meaning: Enteral nutrition timing, nutritional screening, and feeding tube selection in AIS.

units.meaning: Enteral diet should be started within 7 days of admission after AIS.
Nutritional screening with a validated tool is recommended early, preferably within 48 hours of admission.
NG tubes are reasonable initially for dysphagic AIS; PEG placement is appropriate if inability to swallow persists >2-3 weeks.
Evidence: FOOD trial and observational data - early enteral nutrition reduces in-hospital complications.
Evidence: nutritional risk screening early in hospitalization enables timely intervention and reduces malnutrition-related outcomes.
Evidence: FOOD trial arm - NG feeding is preferred initially; PEG is appropriate for long-term anticipated tube feeding.
Nutritional support and feeding access must be planned early to prevent malnutrition-related complications in dysphagic AIS.

5.4.meaning: DVT/VTE prophylaxis in AIS - IPC, subcutaneous heparin, elastic stockings.

units.meaning: In impaired-mobility AIS without IPC contraindications, IPC in addition to routine care is recommended over routine care alone to reduce DVT.
In impaired-mobility AIS, prophylactic-dose subcutaneous UFH or LMWH is reasonable to reduce VTE.
Benefit of prophylactic heparin over no prophylactic heparin for overall survival is not well established.
Benefit of LMWH over UFH for DVT prevention in selected patients is uncertain.
Elastic compression stockings cause HARM - skin breakdown, ulceration, tissue necrosis - and should not be used.
Evidence: CLOTS 3 trial - IPC reduced DVT incidence and mortality in immobile stroke patients.
Evidence: PREVAIL and other trials - subcutaneous heparin reduces VTE events.
Evidence: meta-analyses show VTE reduction but unclear mortality benefit.
Evidence: PREVAIL suggested LMWH reduced DVT more than UFH but bleeding rates were similar; clinical benefit is uncertain.
Evidence: CLOTS 1 and CLOTS 2 trials - graduated stockings did not reduce DVT and caused skin complications.
IPC is the foundation of DVT prophylaxis in immobile AIS patients; subcutaneous heparin is a reasonable addition; elastic stockings should not be used.

5.5.meaning: Post-stroke depression screening and pharmacologic/nonpharmacologic treatment.

units.meaning: A structured depression inventory is recommended for routine post-stroke depression screening; optimal timing uncertain.
In diagnosed PSD, treatment with antidepressants and/or nonpharmacologic interventions (psychotherapy, noninvasive brain stimulation, acupuncture) is recommended.
Evidence: PSD affects a substantial fraction of stroke survivors; structured screening tools improve detection.
Evidence: meta-analyses show both SSRIs and psychosocial interventions improve PSD symptoms.
Post-stroke depression is common and undertreated; routine screening and combined treatment improve outcomes.

5.6.meaning: Palliative care referral, prophylactic antibiotics, and indwelling bladder catheters.

units.meaning: Referral to palliative care is reasonable for select AIS patients and families.
Routine prophylactic antibiotics are NOT beneficial for improving functional outcome.
Routine indwelling bladder catheter placement should NOT be performed - CAUTI risk.
Evidence: palliative care consultation improves family-centered care and goals-of-care alignment in severe stroke.
Evidence: STROKE-INF and PASS trials - prophylactic antibiotics did not improve functional outcome.
Evidence: observational data - routine indwelling catheters increase catheter-associated UTI rates.
General hospital care must avoid low-value/harmful routines (prophylactic antibiotics, routine catheters) while ensuring appropriate palliative support.

5.7.meaning: Early rehabilitation assessment and delivery, SSRIs for motor recovery, and very-early mobilization.

units.meaning: In-hospital formal interdisciplinary rehabilitation assessment and provision at individualized intensity is recommended for functional recovery.
SSRIs are NOT effective for improving motor recovery or functional skills.
High-dose very-early mobilization within 24 hours of stroke onset is NOT recommended and may be HARMFUL.
Evidence: Stroke Unit Trialists' Collaboration - early interdisciplinary rehabilitation integrated with stroke-unit care improves outcomes.
Evidence: FOCUS, AFFINITY, and EFFECTS trials - fluoxetine did not improve motor function or disability after stroke.
Evidence: AVERT trial - very early high-dose mobilization reduced favorable outcomes at 3 months.
Early individualized rehabilitation improves recovery; very-early high-intensity mobilization and SSRIs are not beneficial.

section_6.title: In-Hospital Management of AIS: Treatment of Acute Complications
section_6.meaning: Management of brain swelling, malignant edema, hydrocephalus, and post-stroke seizures during the acute hospitalization.

6.1.meaning: Shared decision-making, close monitoring, and early transfer for patients at risk of malignant brain edema.

units.meaning: In patients at risk for malignant brain edema, early discussion of care options and potential outcomes with patients/families is recommended.
Close neurologic monitoring for worsening during the first 72–96 hours is recommended in patients with large territorial hemispheric or cerebellar infarction.
Early transfer to a center with neurosurgical expertise is recommended for patients at risk of malignant edema.
Evidence: early goals-of-care discussions improve alignment with patient preferences in severe stroke with life-threatening edema risk.
Evidence: peak edema and herniation risk after large hemispheric/cerebellar infarction occur within 72–96 hours; monitoring enables timely intervention.
Evidence: access to decompressive surgery improves outcomes; early transfer prevents delays when deterioration occurs.
Large hemispheric and cerebellar infarctions require proactive monitoring, family discussion, and neurosurgical availability during the high-risk window.

6.2.meaning: Osmotic therapy, glibenclamide, hypothermia, barbiturates, and corticosteroids for cerebral edema.

units.meaning: Osmotic therapy (mannitol or hypertonic saline) may be considered as a bridge to surgical decompression or for clinical deterioration from cerebral edema.
IV glibenclamide is NOT beneficial for preventing or treating malignant brain edema.
Therapeutic hypothermia, barbiturates, and corticosteroids are NOT recommended and are HARMFUL for treatment of cerebral edema after AIS.
Evidence: observational data support transient ICP reduction with osmotic agents; no RCT shows outcome benefit but used as bridge.
Evidence: CHARM trial — IV glibenclamide did not reduce midline shift or improve functional outcome in large hemispheric stroke.
Evidence: ICTuS 2/3 and other trials — hypothermia increased complications without outcome benefit; steroids showed increased harm in ischemic stroke.
Osmotic therapy is a temporizing bridge; glibenclamide offers no benefit; hypothermia, barbiturates, and corticosteroids cause harm.

6.3.meaning: Decompressive hemicraniectomy for malignant MCA edema, stratified by age and post-tPA status.

units.meaning: Decreased level of consciousness attributable to brain swelling is a trigger for urgent surgical evaluation.
In patients ≤60 years with malignant MCA territory infarction deteriorating within 48 hours, decompressive hemicraniectomy is recommended to reduce mortality.
In patients >60 years with malignant MCA territory infarction, decompressive hemicraniectomy may be considered to reduce mortality, acknowledging increased risk of severe disability.
Decompressive craniectomy is reasonable and safe in patients who received IV thrombolysis.
Evidence: observational data — decline in consciousness precedes herniation and identifies patients who benefit most from decompression.
Evidence: DECIMAL, DESTINY, HAMLET pooled analysis — decompressive craniectomy within 48h in ≤60y reduced mortality and improved functional outcome.
Evidence: DESTINY II — craniectomy in >60y reduced mortality but most survivors had mRS 4–5.
Evidence: observational series show no excess bleeding complications when craniectomy follows IV thrombolysis.
Decompressive craniectomy saves lives in malignant MCA edema; benefit is strongest ≤60y and must be weighed against severe disability in older patients.

6.4.meaning: Ventriculostomy and suboccipital craniectomy for cerebellar infarction with mass effect.

units.meaning: Ventriculostomy is recommended for obstructive hydrocephalus from cerebellar infarction, accompanied by or followed by suboccipital decompression.
Suboccipital decompressive craniectomy is recommended for cerebellar infarction with volume ≥35 mL or brainstem compression causing neurologic deterioration.
Evidence: observational studies — ventriculostomy alone without decompression risks upward herniation; combined approach recommended.
Evidence: observational series — suboccipital craniectomy for large cerebellar infarction reduces mortality and frequently yields good functional outcome.
Cerebellar infarction with mass effect or hydrocephalus is a neurosurgical emergency; decompression yields life-saving and often functionally excellent outcomes.

6.5.meaning: Treatment of unprovoked post-stroke seizures and absence of prophylaxis benefit.

units.meaning: Antiseizure medications are recommended for unprovoked seizures after AIS, as with other causes of acute symptomatic seizures.
Prophylactic antiseizure medications are NOT recommended for patients without seizures after AIS.
Evidence: treatment decisions extrapolated from general epilepsy literature; recurrent unprovoked seizures warrant chronic therapy.
Evidence: no trial evidence supports prophylaxis; potential harm from drug side effects and interactions.
Treat unprovoked post-stroke seizures; do not give prophylactic antiseizure medications.

table_4.title: Table 4. Examples of Disabling Neurologic Deficits
table_4.meaning: Examples of deficits that should be considered disabling for IV thrombolysis decision-making in mild stroke.

units.meaning: Complete hemianopia is an example of a disabling deficit even if NIHSS is low.
Severe aphasia is a disabling deficit.
Visuospatial neglect is a disabling deficit.
Weakness limiting sustained effort against gravity or preventing ambulation is disabling.
Loss of functional use of the hand is disabling.
Severe dysarthria causing unintelligible speech is disabling.
Any deficit the patient or clinician judges disabling relative to baseline occupation/function qualifies.

table_5.title: Table 5. Treatment of Symptomatic Intracranial Hemorrhage After IV Thrombolysis
table_5.meaning: Stepwise protocol for managing symptomatic ICH occurring within 24 hours of IV thrombolysis.

units.meaning: Stop IV thrombolytic infusion immediately when sICH is suspected.
Obtain emergent noncontrast head CT to confirm hemorrhage.
Send CBC, PT/PTT/INR, fibrinogen, and type and cross immediately.
Administer cryoprecipitate 10 units IV to replete fibrinogen; additional dose if fibrinogen <150 mg/dL.
Consider tranexamic acid 1000 mg IV over 10 min or ε-aminocaproic acid 4–5 g IV over 1 hour.
Consult neurosurgery and hematology.
Manage blood pressure per ICH protocols to reduce expansion risk.

table_6.title: Table 6. Treatment of Orolingual Angioedema Associated With IV Alteplase
table_6.meaning: Management protocol for orolingual angioedema complicating IV alteplase administration.

units.meaning: Stop alteplase infusion and any ACE inhibitors.
Assess airway; intubate if significant tongue/laryngeal edema present.
Administer methylprednisolone 125 mg IV.
Administer diphenhydramine 50 mg IV.
Administer ranitidine 50 mg IV or famotidine 20 mg IV.
If edema continues to progress, administer epinephrine 0.1% 0.3 mL SC or by nebulizer 0.5 mL.
Icatibant (bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist) or C1 esterase inhibitor may be considered.

table_7.title: Table 7. IV Thrombolysis Treatment Protocol
table_7.meaning: Alteplase and tenecteplase dosing, administration, and monitoring requirements for IV thrombolysis.

units.meaning: Alteplase 0.9 mg/kg IV (maximum 90 mg total dose).
Alteplase 10% of total dose as IV bolus over 1 minute.
Alteplase remaining 90% as IV infusion over 60 minutes.
Tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg IV (maximum 25 mg) as single bolus.
Admit to ICU or stroke unit for close monitoring for at least 24 hours after IV thrombolysis.
Monitor blood pressure every 15 min for 2h, then every 30 min for 6h, then every hour for 16h.
Perform neurologic examinations on the same schedule as blood pressure monitoring.
Withhold aspirin, other antiplatelets, and anticoagulants for 24 hours after IV thrombolysis.
Delay placement of nasogastric tubes, indwelling bladder catheters, and intra-arterial pressure catheters until clinically appropriate.
Obtain follow-up CT or MRI at 24 hours before starting antiplatelet or anticoagulant therapy.
Maintain blood pressure <180/105 mm Hg for at least 24 hours after IV thrombolysis.

table_8.title: Table 8. Management of Other Thrombolysis Situations
table_8.meaning: Additional guidance for thrombolysis in specific situations beyond the standard treatment protocol.

units.meaning: Recent major surgery within 14 days — balance bleeding risk at surgical site against stroke disability.
Recent GI/GU hemorrhage within 21 days — weigh rebleeding risk individually.
Recent myocardial infarction — alteplase reasonable for non-STEMI and most STEMI; balance pericarditis risk.
Pregnancy — IV thrombolysis may be considered for moderate-severe stroke when anticipated benefit outweighs uterine bleeding risk.
Menstruation — IV thrombolysis probably indicated; monitor for increased vaginal bleeding.

table_9.title: Table 9. Trials of Dual Antiplatelet Therapy After Minor Stroke or High-Risk TIA
table_9.meaning: Summary of CHANCE, POINT, and THALES trials supporting DAPT for minor stroke or high-risk TIA.

units.meaning: CHANCE trial — clopidogrel + aspirin for 21 days after minor stroke/high-risk TIA reduced recurrent stroke vs aspirin alone.
POINT trial — clopidogrel + aspirin reduced ischemic events but increased bleeding; benefit largely confined to first 21 days.
THALES trial — ticagrelor + aspirin for 30 days reduced stroke/death vs aspirin alone in minor stroke/high-risk TIA; more severe bleeding.
Pooled interpretation — 21 days of clopidogrel+aspirin (or 30 days of ticagrelor+aspirin) is the optimal duration balancing benefit and bleeding.

figure_3.title: Figure 3. EVT Eligibility Algorithm
figure_3.meaning: Decision algorithm for endovascular thrombectomy eligibility by time from last known well, imaging findings, NIHSS, ASPECTS, and circulation.

units.meaning: 0–6 hours from LKW with anterior LVO, NIHSS ≥6, ASPECTS ≥3 → EVT recommended (Class 1).
6–24 hours from LKW with anterior LVO → EVT recommended if imaging (CTP/DWI) mismatch meets DAWN or DEFUSE-3 criteria.
Basilar artery occlusion within 24 hours with NIHSS ≥10 → EVT recommended.
Distal/medium vessel occlusions (M2/M3, A2/A3, P2/P3) → benefit uncertain; consider individually.
ASPECTS <3 (large core) → EVT may be considered based on SELECT2, RESCUE-Japan LIMIT, ANGEL-ASPECT.
Beyond 24 hours from LKW → EVT not recommended in current guideline framework.

figure_4.title: Figure 4. DAPT for Minor Stroke and TIA
figure_4.meaning: Decision algorithm for starting DAPT after minor stroke (NIHSS ≤3) or high-risk TIA (ABCD² ≥4).

units.meaning: Eligibility: noncardioembolic minor stroke NIHSS ≤3 OR high-risk TIA (ABCD² ≥4) within 24 hours of onset.
Start DAPT within 24 hours of symptom onset.
Regimen: clopidogrel 300–600 mg loading dose, then 75 mg daily, plus aspirin 81 mg daily.
Continue DAPT for 21 days, then transition to single antiplatelet therapy.
Do NOT use DAPT if recent intracranial hemorrhage or active bleeding.
